study_notes.title: The Axial Skeleton: The Rib Cage
study_notes.introduction: The rib cage is a vital part of the axial skeleton, forming a protective, bony shield around the thoracic cavity. It is not just for protection; it acts as a flexible, mechanical pump that powers the respiratory system.

structural_anatomy.sternum: A flat, dagger-shaped bone in the center of the chest. It acts as the primary anchor point for the true ribs at the front.
structural_anatomy.vertebral_attachment: At the back, all 12 pairs of ribs are securely attached to the 12 thoracic vertebrae of the spine.
structural_anatomy.costal_cartilage: Flexible, rubbery tissue connecting the ribs to the sternum. This flexibility is the biological 'key' to breathing; without it, the chest could not expand.

classification_of_ribs.true_ribs: Pairs 1-7 (14 ribs total). They attach directly and independently to the sternum. They are the 'True' anchors of the chest wall.
classification_of_ribs.false_ribs: Pairs 8-10. These ribs do not attach directly to the sternum. They 'piggyback' onto the cartilage of the rib above them.
classification_of_ribs.floating_ribs: Pairs 11-12. These ribs have no attachment at the front (anterior) end. They protect the kidneys and lower abdominal organs from the back.

inhalation.rib_movement: Muscles between the ribs (intercostals) contract, pulling the ribs UP and OUT.
inhalation.effect: The volume of the chest cavity increases, air pressure drops, and air rushes into the lungs.

exhalation.rib_movement: The muscles relax, and the heavy rib cage moves DOWN and INWARD.
exhalation.effect: The chest volume decreases, internal pressure increases, and waste air is forcefully pushed out.

primary_functions.protection: Shields vital cardiovascular and respiratory organs (Heart and Lungs) from physical impact.
primary_functions.respiration: Acts as a mechanical bellows to facilitate the movement of air during inhalation and exhalation.
primary_functions.blood_factory: The flat bones of the rib cage contain active red bone marrow, which is essential for manufacturing red blood cells.
primary_functions.mineral_storage: Like all bones, ribs act as a storage bank for essential Calcium and Phosphorus.

common_disorders_and_safety.fractures: A break in the rib bone. Because ribs are close to soft organs, broken shards can puncture lungs, leading to internal bleeding or lung collapse (pneumothorax).
common_disorders_and_safety.CPR_safety: During CPR, compressions must be applied to the sternum. Pressing too low can snap the fragile 'xiphoid process' (tip of the sternum), potentially damaging the liver.
common_disorders_and_safety.postural_damage: Slouching compresses the chest cavity, restricting the rib cage and preventing the lungs from inflating to their full capacity.

study_tips_for_ple: True ribs (1-7) attach to the breastbone; False ribs (8-10) attach to cartilage; Floating ribs (11-12) attach to nothing at the front.
Intercostal muscles are the 'cables' between the ribs that power the breathing movement.
Cartilage is flexible; solid bone is not. We breathe because our chest is flexible!
